segments.s622-g49: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning “he said/ordered” with the subject following it (بهرام). تا introduces the content/purpose clause here — “that/so that he sit” — and links the command to the following verb. برنشست is the classical past form “sat down / took a seat.”
segments.s622-g50: گرفت is a simple past “he took/seized.” آن زمان is a time phrase “then / at that time.” دست او را is the definite object (او = his/he, را = object marker) “his hand,” and به دست here is a literal prepositional phrase “into (his) hand” — an idiom meaning he took the other man’s hand in his own. Word order places the object plus را before the prepositional phrase.
segments.s622-g51: بیامد نشَست is a two-part narrative: بیامد “he came” (simple past) + نشست “he sat” (past). از بر تخت زر is a classical locative phrasing where از بر + noun means “upon/on” (تخت زر = “golden throne”), so the whole means “he came and took his seat on the golden throne.”
segments.s622-g52: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones/nobles.” به پیش اندرون literally “to/into the presence/inner quarters” (به + پیش + اندرون) describes their movement toward the inner court. با کمر is a compact phrase “with (their) girdles/at the waist” — کمر here means belt/girdle, so it describes how they came (girded).
segments.s622-g53: ببخشید is an archaic/past action-form here (past 3rd singular) meaning 'bestowed/gave' — the initial ب is a poetic prefix found in Ferdowsi. The verb precedes its objects: گنجی (a treasure, object) followed by به + indirect object مرد نیاز 'to/for the needy man' (مرد = 'man', نیاز here = 'need/needy' functioning as an attributive noun).
segments.s622-g54: در تنگ زندان is a locative phrase 'in/within the narrow prison' (در = 'in'; تنگ = 'narrow' describing زندان = 'prison'). گشادند is a plural predicative verb/adjective meaning 'were opened / were made wide' (state-change), and باز functions adverbially here as 'open/wide' (so together 'were opened wide' or 'were opened again/open'). The grammatical subject that 'گشادند' refers to is implied (the prison's bonds/doors).
segments.s622-g55: زمانه is the subject 'the age/era' (or 'the world'); پر از + noun means 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'; از = 'of/from'), so پر از رامش و داد شد = 'became full of tranquility and justice.' شد is the copular/predicative perfective 'became' (past 3rd singular).
segments.s622-g56: دل غمگنان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the hearts of the sorrowful' (دل = 'heart' governed by the following descriptive noun غمگنان 'the sorrowful'). از غم آزاد شد is a verbal clause 'were freed from grief' where آزاد is a predicative adjective 'free' and شد is the past copula 'became/was made.'
segments.s622-g1: چو is an archaic subordinating conjunction meaning 'when'. شد is the past third-person singular of شدن 'to become/happen' and here combines with ساخته کار آتشکده (a noun phrase) to mean 'was completed' or 'came to be made'. ساخته modifies کار ('the work that was made/constructed'), and آتشکده is the head noun 'fire-temple'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: the temporal clause ('When ... was made') precedes the rest.
segments.s622-g2: همان جای is a nominal phrase meaning 'that very place' or 'that place itself' (همان = 'that/the same', جای = 'place'). نوروز و جشن سده are two festivals joined by و; the copular/verb 'became' is understood from the previous clause (شد). The verb is thus omitted here for brevity, typical in classical verse.
segments.s622-g3: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came'. سوی is a preposition 'toward' and governs the place name آذرآبادگان (Azarābādgān). In Persian the verb precedes the prepositional complement in this poetic line, but the sense is straightforward: 'he came toward Azarābādgān.'
segments.s622-g4: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing who came: خود here functions as a possessive/emphatic 'himself'/'his own' (i.e., he himself), followed by و نامداران و آزادگان 'and the renowned and the free-born'. There is no separate verb because it attaches to بیامد earlier; the list is simply objects/companions of that motion.
segments.s622-g5: پرستندگان is the plural noun 'worshippers'. پیش here means 'before' or 'in the presence of' (i.e., before the fire). آذر is 'fire' (here the sacred fire), and شدند is past plural of شدن 'they became/were' used idiomatically to indicate they came to be in that position—overall, 'the worshippers came before the fire.'
segments.s622-g6: همه موبدان 'all the mobeds' (Zoroastrian priests) is the subject; دست بر سر شدن is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'to put one's hand on the head' (a gesture of supplication or reverence). دست = 'hand', بر = 'on/upon', سر = 'head', and شدند is the past plural 'they did so/they put'.
segments.s622-g7: پرستندگان را uses را as the definite-object marker ('upon/for the worshippers'). ببخشید here is best read as the transitive past 'he bestowed/gave' (بخشیدن), so the sequence means 'he gave/bestowed a thing to the worshippers.' چیز is the object 'a thing/something' — indefinite and general.
segments.s622-g8: This short clause describes where/what he did next. وز in classical usage often functions as a prepositional or connective particle (here the exact sense is archaic and does not match the dictionary senses given), آتشکده = 'the fire-temple' and روی بنهاد (بنهاد is past of نهادن 'to place/lay/found', literally 'he placed/put upon') with تیز as an adverb 'swiftly/quickly' (تیز used adverbially). The whole means 'and he swiftly set (it) upon the fire-temple.'
segments.s622-g25: 'چهارم' marks the ordinal ("Fourth:") opening this point. 'هران پیر' (here written together) means "any/each old man" — 'هران' is an indefinite distributive placed before the noun. 'کز' is the relative particle "who/that/from which" linking to the following clause, and 'کارکرد' is a noun "(his) doing/performances" that 'کز' refers to (i.e. "who, from his deeds").
segments.s622-g26: 'فروماند' is a past/resultative participial form meaning "was worn out/left behind" (literally "remained down/worn"). 'وز' here functions as a poetic preposition equivalent to 'از' ("from") linking the cause; none of the provided senses capture that prepositional use. 'روز ننگ و نبرد' is a genitive-like sequence "days (times) of shame and battle" describing what exhausted him.
segments.s622-g27: 'به پنجم' introduces the fifth point ("as fifth/for the fifth"). 'هرانکس که' is the indefinite relative "whoever/anyone who". 'بد با نژاد' is an inverted adjective + noun phrase meaning "bad/ill of lineage" (i.e. "ill-born"); the adjective 'بد' precedes and modifies 'نژاد'.
segments.s622-g28: 'توانگر نکردی' is a negative past action: 'نکردی' (you did not make/do) with object 'توانگر' (to make wealthy/wealthy). The subject is implicit (contextually the ruler/you). 'ازو' = 'of him/from him' and 'هیچ یاد' = 'no remembrance/any memory' — the sentence states that for anyone of bad birth you did not enrich, there should be no memorial of him. Word order places the negative verb before the possessive/object phrase.
segments.s622-g29: 'ششم' marks the sixth item. 'هرکه آمد' is a generic past clause "whoever came" (هرکه = whoever; آمد = came, simple past 3rd sg used generically). 'ز راه دراز' is a prepositional phrase "from a long road/way," describing the origin of the traveller.
segments.s622-g30: 'همی داشت' is a habitual/imperfective construction common in epic/poetic Persian (همی + past-stem) indicating repeated or customary action: "kept/was keeping." The object phrase 'درویشی خویش ش راز' should be read as 'درویشیِ خویشِش راز' — 'درویشی' = "dervishness/poor, ascetic state," 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' together function as the possessive "his own," and 'راز' = "secret." Together: "he kept his dervishhood secret."
segments.s622-g31: 'بدیشان' is a dative expression meaning 'to them/for them.' 'ببخشید' here is the past third-person form of بخشیدن used in the sense "bestowed/gave" (narrative past). 'چندین درم' is the direct object "several dirhams." Word order is dative + verb + object, common in Persian narration.
segments.s622-g32: 'نبد' is the negative copular/auxiliary meaning "was not / did not exist" (here negating the following attribute). 'شاه' is the subject "the king." The sequence 'روزی ز بخشش دژم' is idiomatic: 'روزی' (contextually "fortune/wealth" or "his daily provision") + 'ز بخشش' "from giving" + 'دژم' "sullen/stingy"; together the clause says that the king did not become stingy or lose his fortune on account of giving. Several of the individual lexical senses supplied do not exactly match these poetic uses (so some items above are marked -1).
segments.s622-g33: This is a past clause with the verb نهاد (placed) at the end (common in Persian). غنیمت همه is the object phrase 'all the booty' (غنیمت = booty, همه = all) and بهر لشکر is a beneficiary prepositional phrase 'for the army' (بهر = for; لشکر = army). The subject is omitted (third-person).
segments.s622-g34: نیامدش is an impersonal/personal construction meaning 'it did not please him / it was not fitting' with the enclitic -ش = 'to him'. از آگندن is a prepositional phrase where از introduces the action 'to soil' (آگندن) of the following noun گنج 'treasure'. باد is the archaic/poetic optative/subjunctive particle ('may/should'), here marking the subordinate desirability/appropriateness ('that the treasure be soiled' — which he found unfitting).
segments.s622-g35: بفرمود is a polite/past reporting verb 'he said/ordered' (past, 3rd person). پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. تاج خاقان چین is a genitive chain 'the crown of the khaghan of China' (poetry often omits the ezāfe linking). The noun phrase follows the verb here for stylistic inversion.
segments.s622-g36: که begins a relative clause 'which/that'. پیش آورد is a compound past verb 'brought forward' (پیش 'before/in the presence of' + آورد 'brought'); آورد is the lexical verb 'bring'. مردم پاک‌دین is the agent phrase 'the devout people' (مردم = people, پاک‌دین = devout/pious), with the adjective/epithet after the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s622-g37: گهرها 'the jewels' is the head noun (plural). که بود اندرو is a relative clause 'that were inside' (که = that; بود = was/were, past; اندرو = in/inside). آژده is an adjectival/predicative word here 'terrifying' (poetic/archaic form related to 'terrible/monstrous') and stands with the relative clause to describe the situation or thing found there; the short clause omits an explicit copula or object, a common poetic compression.
segments.s622-g38: بکندند is the main verb 'they dug up/they uprooted' (past, 3rd plural). و is the coordinating 'and'. دیوار آتشکده is a noun phrase 'the wall of the fire-temple' (دیوار = wall; آتشکده = fire-temple); in context this noun phrase is coordinated with the previously understood object(s) of بکندند, so the verb applies to both.
segments.s622-g39: به زر و به گوهر uses the preposition به instrumentally 'with/using' twice for emphasis ('with gold and with gems'). بیاراستند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they adorned/ornamented' and takes the preceding material phrases as instruments.
segments.s622-g40: سر تخت آذر is a genitive chain meaning 'the top/head of the throne of fire' (سر = top/head; تخت = throne; آذر = fire). بپیراستند is another past, 3rd-plural verb 'they adorned/they arrayed' (similar in meaning to بیاراستند); the verb again comes at the end and agrees with the plural agent.
segments.s622-g57: ز هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase (ز + determiner phrase) meaning 'from every land/realm' placed before the affected nouns. The compound object رنج و غم ('suffering and sorrow') follows, and دور کرد is the verbal complex: دور (an adverb/adjectival predicative meaning 'away/removed') plus کرد, the past tense 3rd-person singular of کردن, here functioning as 'made/caused to be distant' (i.e., 'drove away').
segments.s622-g58: ز بهر بزرگان is an archaic/poetic beneficiary phrase meaning 'for the sake of the great ones / for the nobles' (ز بهر functioning like 'for'). یکی سور کرد places یکی ('one, a single') before سور ('banquet, feast') for emphasis; کرد is again the past 3rd-singular 'made/held'. The overall structure is SOV-like: beneficiary phrase + numeral + noun + verb = 'he held one feast for the nobles.'
segments.s622-g59: بدان is an archaic locative/pointing form (be + an/دان) meaning 'at/to that' or 'at that place/occasion' and here links to سور ('banquet') to yield 'at that banquet'. هرکس که بشتافتی is a relative clause: هرکس ('whoever') + که (relative particle 'who/that') + بشتافتی (past, 2nd-person singular 'you hastened/ran'); the clause modifies which people are meant ('whoever you hurried to/approached').
segments.s622-g60: همه is 'all/everyone' (here indicating that the following applied to everyone). خلعت مهتری is a noun phrase where خلعت ('robe of honour') is qualified by مهتری (an attributive sense 'of preeminence/chiefship'), i.e. 'the robe marking preeminence'. یافتی is the past 2nd-person singular of یافتن ('to obtain/find') and is used here in the sense 'received/obtained' (poetically implying that those people came away with such a robe).
segments.s622-g9: خرامان is an adverbial/descriptive participle meaning ‘with a proud/stately gait’ placed before the verb for poetic effect; بیامد is a simple past verb ‘came’; به + شهر صطخر is the prepositional destination ('to the city of Istakhr'). Word order is Adverb — Verb — Prepositional phrase.
segments.s622-g10: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'شهر صطخر'. شاهنشهان را uses را as a marker attaching the emperors as the affected/beneficiary group ('for the emperors'); the sequence ب + دان + بود is a poetic/archaic verbal construction meaning 'was considered' (ب = to / prepositional element, دان = 'to consider'), and فخر is the predicate noun 'pride'. Overall: 'which was considered a pride for the shahanshahs.'
segments.s622-g11: پراگنده functions as a substantive/adjective 'widespread, broadly spread' and is followed by a material phrase introduced by از ('made of/from'); چرم گاوان میش specifies the material 'leather of oxen (and) ewes'. The noun-of-material phrase follows the adjective as is common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s622-g12: که starts a relative clause referring back to the 'پراگنده'. بر + پشت + پیلان is a locative phrase 'on the backs of the elephants'; همی + راند is the epic/habitual imperfective marker ('used to drive' / 'drove repeatedly' — here rendered as a past narrative continuous), and پیش is an adverb 'ahead / before' indicating that it was driven in front. The subject is omitted and carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s622-g13: This is a numeric listing: هزار (thousand) + صد (hundred) + شست (sixty) followed by the unit قنطار and the past copula بود 'was/were'. The two و's join the numerals. Note: the word شست here is an older/poetic spelling for 'sixty' (شصت).
segments.s622-g14: درم begins a new clause 'dirhams'; بو here is an archaic/poetic form related to 'بود' (the past copula) and functions as 'there was' (treated as irregular in the available sense list); ازو indicates possession or source 'of him/from him'; نیز 'also'; و joins items; دینار 'dinar' and the final بود is the past copula. Together: 'there were dirhams of his as well, and dinars.'
segments.s622-g15: که begins another relative clause. بر پهلوی = 'in Pahlavi' (the Pahlavi language/form). موبد پارسی is a noun phrase 'the Persian mobed' (موبد = Zoroastrian priest, modified by پارسی). The preposition بر + language indicates 'in the language of...'.
segments.s622-g16: همی is the habitual/continuous marker 'would/used to'; نام بردیش is the verbal phrase 'to name / to mention' with the enclitic ش indicating a third‑person object ('he called him / he named him'); پیداوسی is the proper name/epithet given. Grammatically: 'he (the mobed) would name him Peydausi.' Note: بردیش is a poetic/older formation of برد + ش (object suffix).
segments.s622-g41: وزان is a poetic contraction (و + زان) meaning “from that / from there.” جایگه is a noun “place.” شد here is a past, intransitive/predicative verb used idiomatically as “proceeded/turned” (poetic usage rather than the literal ‘become’). سوی marks direction and precedes the destination طیسفون (Ctesiphon). The whole phrase means “from that place he proceeded toward Ctesiphon.”
segments.s622-g42: که introduces a relative/locative clause (“where / in which”). نرسی بد and موبد رهنمون here are proper names/titles (Narsibad and the mobed Rahnamun); in older poetic Persian the copula is often omitted, so the clause effectively says “where Narsibad and the mobed Rahnamun (were).” و simply links the two names.
segments.s622-g43: پذیره (reception) plus the past plural شد(ند) and the enclitic -ش together form a causative/receiver construction meaning “they received/accepted him” (پذیره شدند + -ش = “they made reception of him / they received him”). همه مهتران is the noun phrase “all the mahterān/elders/chiefs.” Note the verb phrase appears before the explicit noun phrase, a common ordering in epic poetry.
segments.s622-g44: This coordinated noun phrase names those who received him: بزرگان ایران = “the great ones of Iran,” and کنداوران names another group (here treated as a group-name). و links the two coordinated groups; the whole phrase functions as the agent/topic tied to the reception verb in the previous segment.
segments.s622-g45: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” نرسی here is a personal name (Narsibad) rather than the verb ‘to arrive’ (so treated as a proper noun). بدید is the past tense “saw.” آن سر و تاج شاه is the definite object “that head and the king’s crown.” The clause reads: “When Narsibad saw that head and crown of the king...”
segments.s622-g46: This continues the list of things seen: درفش دلفروز is the named banner (درفش = “banner/standard,” دلفروز an epithet/name “heart‑illuminating” attached to it). و connects the next item; چندان is a quantifying adjective “so many / such great” modifying سپاه “army/host.” In poetry objects can be listed in apposition like this.
segments.s622-g47: پیاده شد is a compound verbal phrase meaning “he dismounted / went on foot” (پیاده used adverbially/adjectivally with شد). و links to the following clause. برد here functions as the past of بردن (“brought / presented / conducted”) with نماز (“prayer”) as its object; پیشش marks location/presence “before him.” The clause means roughly “and the prayer was brought before him” (subject/agent is supplied by the next segment).
segments.s622-g48: This gives the agents/participants: بزرگان “the great ones / nobles” and هم موبد سرفراز “and also the honoured/valiant mobed (priest).” هم = “also,” and the epithet سرفراز either describes the mobed or serves as a proper epithet/name. These nouns are the logical performers or participants in the acted-upon نماز from the previous segment.
segments.s622-g17: بیاورد is a past third-person singular/action verb meaning ‘brought’ (classical past). پس is a discourse adverb ‘then/thereupon’ placed after the verb for sequencing. مشکهای is the plural construct ‘musk(s) of…’ (object), and ادیم is an adjective/epithet ‘earthly, of the ground’ qualifying مشکهای; the phrase as a whole means ‘he brought the musks of the earth.’
segments.s622-g18: بگسترد is another past/habitual action ‘spread’ (he spread [them]). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ شادان is an adverbial/adjective ‘joyful, rejoicing’ describing the manner. برو is here another verb ‘went’ (he went/then went) linking to the following verb ریخت; ریخت is used as a past action ‘poured’ (literally ‘he poured/let fall’), so the clause reads ‘and, joyful, he went and poured (silver).’
segments.s622-g19: به ره is a prepositional phrase ‘on the road / on the way’ (به + ره). بر هران پل places the noun phrase ‘on those/each of the bridges’ (بر = on/upon; هران here ‘each/those’). که introduces a relative clause and ویران is the predicate adjective ‘ruined’; بدید is the past tense verb ‘he saw.’ Word order: prepositional place phrase → noun phrase → relative clause with verb at the end (classical past).
segments.s622-g20: رباطی is the noun ‘a caravanserai/inn’ (here a place he encounters). که is the relative particle ‘that/which.’ از marks source ‘from’ or ‘by’ and کاروانان is the plural ‘the caravans’ (instrument/source). شنید is the past verb ‘he heard’ (here ‘heard of/heard from’), so the clause means ‘a caravanserai which he had heard of from the caravans.’
segments.s622-g21: ز here is the poetic form of ‘from/away (from)’ marking separation. گیتی ‘world’ with دگر ‘other/apart’ forms the adverbial phrase ‘apart from the world.’ هرکه is the indefinite ‘whoever,’ درویش is the predicate noun ‘a dervish/ascetic,’ and بود is the past copula ‘was.’ The order places the adverbial before the subject phrase, common in classical style.
segments.s622-g22: وگر is a conditional particle ‘if / but if.’ نانش ‘his bread’ (= his livelihood) is the subject/object, از introduces the source ‘from,’ کوشش ‘effort/exertion’ is the source phrase, and خوی + ش together function reflexively/possessively as ‘his own’ (here marked by the split forms in the dataset). بود is again the past copula ‘was.’ The clause reads ‘and if his bread was from his own exertion.’
segments.s622-g23: سدیگر is a compound meaning ‘another / the other (one)’ (here introducing a new action). به is the preposition ‘into/at/with’ marking relation to کپان (here ‘kapan’ = snares, nets, or sacks depending on context). بسختید is an archaic/poetic past form (here interpreted as ‘made firm / fastened / fixed’); it is an older conjugation and functions as the main past verb of the clause. The omitted word سیم in the segment functions as the object ‘silver’ that was fixed/placed.
segments.s622-g24: زن بیوه is a noun phrase ‘a widow woman’ (زن = woman/wife, بیوه = widow). و is the coordinator ‘and.’ کودکان یتیم is the plural noun phrase ‘orphan children’ (کودکان = children, یتیم = orphan). The phrase lists the beneficiaries or recipients of the previously mentioned silver/aid.

sentences.s622-s25: Bahram ordered him to sit; when he sat down, he took his hand in his own.
sentences.s622-s26: He came and sat upon the golden throne; the great men approached the inner court, girded at the waist.
sentences.s622-s27: He bestowed a treasure on the needy man; the narrow prison's confines were opened wide again.
sentences.s622-s28: The age became full of peace and justice; the hearts of the sorrowful were freed from grief.
sentences.s622-s1: When the work of the fire-temple had been completed, that place became the site of Nowruz and the Sadeh festival.
sentences.s622-s2: He came toward Azarābādgān — himself, the renowned, and the free-born.
sentences.s622-s3: The worshippers approached before the fire, and all the mobeds put their hands upon their heads.
sentences.s622-s4: He bestowed something upon the worshippers, and quickly set it upon the fire-temple.
sentences.s622-s13: Fourth: any old man who, worn out by his exertions, was spent from days of shame and battle.
sentences.s622-s14: Fifth: whoever is ill-born — whom you have not made wealthy — let there be no remembrance of him.
sentences.s622-s15: Sixth: whoever came from a long road kept his dervishness secret.
sentences.s622-s16: He bestowed upon them several dirhams; the king did not lose any fortune through giving (he was not stingy).
sentences.s622-s17: He set aside all the booty for the army; it did not seem fitting to him that the treasure be soiled.
sentences.s622-s18: Then he commanded the crown of the Khaghan of China to be brought forward by the devout people.
sentences.s622-s19: They dug up the jewels that were inside — a terrifying thing — and the wall of the fire-temple.
sentences.s622-s20: They adorned it with gold and with gems; they arrayed the top of the throne of fire.
sentences.s622-s29: He drove suffering and sorrow away from every land; for the great ones he held a single feast.
sentences.s622-s30: At that banquet, whoever you hurried to, everyone received the robe of preeminence.
sentences.s622-s5: He came striding into the city of Istakhr, which was a pride to the emperors.
sentences.s622-s6: A wide-spread covering of ox and ewe leather, which he drove forward upon the backs of elephants.
sentences.s622-s7: There were a thousand and a hundred and sixty qintars; there were dirhams from him as well, and dinars.
sentences.s622-s8: which, in the Pahlavi of the Persian mobed, the name he used to give was 'Peydausi'.
sentences.s622-s21: From there he turned toward Ctesiphon, where Narsibad and the mobed Rahnamun (were present).
sentences.s622-s22: All the mahterān received him: the great men of Iran and the Kandāvarān.
sentences.s622-s23: When Narsibad saw that head and the king’s crown, the Dalfroz banner and so great an army,
sentences.s622-s24: He dismounted, and before him the prayer of the great men—and also the honourable mobed—was presented.
sentences.s622-s9: Then he brought earthly musks, spread them, and, joyful, went and poured out silver.
sentences.s622-s10: On the road he saw those bridges ruined; a caravanserai which he had heard of from the caravans.
sentences.s622-s11: Whoever was a dervish, cut off from worldly life; and if his bread came from his own exertion.
sentences.s622-s12: He set another (something) among the traps and firmly fixed silver — (for) widowed women and orphaned children.
